subject: Physics
q: A ray of light of intensity $\mathrm{I}$ is incident on a parallel glass slab at point $\mathrm{A}$ as shown in diagram. It undergoes partial reflection and refraction. At each reflection, $25 \%$ of incident energy is reflected. The rays $\mathrm{AB}$ and $\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ undergo interference. The ratio of $\mathrm{I}_{\max }$ and $\mathrm{I}_{\min }$ is :
question: A ray of light of intensity $\mathrm{I}$ is incident on a parallel glass slab at point $\mathrm{A}$ as shown in diagram. It undergoes partial reflection and refraction. At each reflection, $25 \%$ of incident energy is reflected. The rays $\mathrm{AB}$ and $\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ undergo interference. The ratio of $\mathrm{I}_{\max }$ and $\mathrm{I}_{\min }$ is :
solution: From figure $I_1=\frac{I}{4}$ and $I_2=\frac{9 I}{64}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{I_2}{I_1}=\frac{9}{16}$ By using $\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{\frac{I_2}{I_1}}+1}{\sqrt{\frac{I_2}{I_1}}-1}\right)^2$ $=\left(\frac{\sqrt{\frac{9}{16}}+1}{\sqrt{\frac{9}{16}}-1}\right)^2=\frac{49}{1}$

subject: Physics
q: A ray of light passing through an equilateral prism is having velocity $2.12 \times 10^8$ m/s in the prism material, then the minimum angle of deviation is _________ degrees.
question: A ray of light passing through an equilateral prism is having velocity $2.12 \times 10^8$ m/s in the prism material, then the minimum angle of deviation is _________ degrees.
solution:
Velocity of light in vacuum, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

Velocity of light in the prism, $v = 2.12 \times 10^8$ m/s

Refractive index of the prism material, $\mu = \dfrac{c}{v} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{2.12 \times 10^8} \approx 1.414 \approx \sqrt{2}$

For an equilateral prism, the angle of the prism $A = 60^\circ$.

Using the prism formula:

$\mu = \dfrac{\sin\left(\dfrac{A + \delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)}$

$\sqrt{2} = \dfrac{\sin\left(\dfrac{60^\circ + \delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin(30^\circ)}$

$\sqrt{2} = \dfrac{\sin\left(30^\circ + \dfrac{\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{2}}$

$\sin\left(30^\circ + \dfrac{\delta_m}{2}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

$30^\circ + \dfrac{\delta_m}{2} = 45^\circ$

$\dfrac{\delta_m}{2} = 15^\circ$

$\delta_m = 30^\circ$

Answer: $30$

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in front of convex mirror with principal axis coinciding each other. The distance between the lens and mirror is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. A point object is placed on principal axis at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the convex lens. The image formed by combination coincides the object itself. The focal length of the convex mirror is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: A convex lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in front of convex mirror with principal axis coinciding each other. The distance between the lens and mirror is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. A point object is placed on principal axis at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the convex lens. The image formed by combination coincides the object itself. The focal length of the convex mirror is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
For lens 1v-1u=1f

⇒1v-1-60=120⇒1v+160=120

v=30 cm

For the final image to be formed on the object itself, after refraction from lens the ray should meet the mirror perpendicularly and the image by lens should be on the centre of curvature of mirror.

R=30-10=20 cm.

Now the focal length of mirror fm=R2=10 cm

If its focal length in liquid is <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow></msub></math> , then the ratio <math><mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow></mrow></math> is closest to the integer:
solution:
1fa=μgμa-11R1-1R2

⇒fafm=μgμm-1μgμa-1=1.501.42-11.501-1=0.081.920.5

subject: Physics
q: A light ray is incident, at an incident angle θ1, on the system of two plane mirrors M1 and M2 having an inclination angle 75° between them (as shown in figure). After reflecting from mirror M1 it gets reflected back by the mirror M2 with an angle of reflection 30°. The total deviation of the ray will be _____ degree.
question: A light ray is incident, at an incident angle θ1, on the system of two plane mirrors M1 and M2 having an inclination angle 75° between them (as shown in figure). After reflecting from mirror M1 it gets reflected back by the mirror M2 with an angle of reflection 30°. The total deviation of the ray will be _____ degree.
solution:
From the above figure, i2=30°,

Now, 

∠OBA+∠ABR+∠RBP=180°⇒2∠OBA+60°=180°⇒∠OBA=60°

In triangle OAB,

∠OBA+∠AOB+∠OAB=180°⇒60°+75°+∠OAB=180°⇒∠OAB=45°

And,

∠OAB+∠BAI+∠IAQ=180°⇒2∠OAB+2i1=180°⇒i1=45°

Now the total deviation of the ray will be,

δ=360°-2i1+i2⇒δ=360°-230°+45°δ=210°

subject: Physics
q: In the diagram given below, there are three lenses formed. Considering negligible thickness of each of them as compared to $\left|R_1\right|$ and $\left|R_2\right|$, i.e., the radii of curvature for upper and lower surfaces of the glass lens, the power of the combination is
question: In the diagram given below, there are three lenses formed. Considering negligible thickness of each of them as compared to $\left|R_1\right|$ and $\left|R_2\right|$, i.e., the radii of curvature for upper and lower surfaces of the glass lens, the power of the combination is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow p_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{p}_1+\mathrm{p}_2+\mathrm{p}_3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_1=\left(\frac{4}{3}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_1=\left(\frac{1}{3\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_2=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_2=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_3=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)=-\frac{1}{3\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right) \\ & =-\frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right)\end{aligned}$

a→=sinθi^-cosθj^

b→=sinθi^+cosθj^

c→=j^

a→-2a→·c→c→=sinθi^+cosθj^

subject: Physics
q: You are asked to design a shaving mirror assuming that a person keeps it at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from his face and views the magnified image of the face at the closest comfortable distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The radius of curvature of the mirror would then be:
question: You are asked to design a shaving mirror assuming that a person keeps it at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from his face and views the magnified image of the face at the closest comfortable distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The radius of curvature of the mirror would then be:
solution:
If AB is the position of the face of man then A'B' is the position of the image of a face. As the image is formed at 25 cm from the object.∴ From concave mirror image is 15cm behind the mirror. So, u=-10 cm, v= +15 cm
⇒1f=1u+1v⇒1f=1-10+115=-3+230
⇒f=-30 cm. So, the radius of curvature,=60 cm

subject: Physics
q: Given is a thin convex lens of glass (refractive index $\mu$ ) and each side having radius of curvature $R$. One side is polished for complete reflection. At what distance from the lens, an object be placed on the optic axis so that the image gets formed on the object itself ?
question: Given is a thin convex lens of glass (refractive index $\mu$ ) and each side having radius of curvature $R$. One side is polished for complete reflection. At what distance from the lens, an object be placed on the optic axis so that the image gets formed on the object itself ?
solution: $\begin{aligned} -\frac{1}{f_{e q}} & =\frac{2}{f_l}-\frac{1}{f_m} \\ & =2(\mu-1) \frac{2}{R}+\frac{2}{R} \\ -\frac{1}{f_{e q}} & =\frac{2(2 \mu-1)}{R} \\ f_{e q} & =-\frac{R}{2(2 \mu-1)} \end{aligned}$ For concave mirror, object should be at $2 f$ for the image to be at same point $\text { Distance }=\frac{R}{(2 \mu-1)}$

Telescope produces virtual, erect image of an object situated far away from it at least distance of distinct vision from the eye lens. In this condition the Magnifying Power of the Galilean Telescope should be :
question: The focal lengths of objective lens and eye lens of a Galilean Telescope are respectively 30 cm and 3.0 cm. Telescope produces virtual, erect image of an object situated far away from it at least distance of distinct vision from the eye lens. In this condition the Magnifying Power of the Galilean Telescope should be :
solution:
MP = - f⁡ 0 f⁡ e 1 + f⁡ e D

      = - 3 0 - 3 1 + - 3 2 5

      = + 11.2

q: Consider a light ray travelling in air is incident into a medium of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></msqrt></math>. The incident angle is twice that of refracting angle. Then, the angle of incidence will be
question: Consider a light ray travelling in air is incident into a medium of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></msqrt></math>. The incident angle is twice that of refracting angle. Then, the angle of incidence will be
solution:
Let angle of incidence be θ, then angle of refraction θ2. From Snell's law, we can write,

1×sinθ=2n×sinθ2, 2sinθ2cosθ2=2nsinθ2

cosθ2=n2 ⇒θ=2cos-1n2

A straight line passing through the origin and making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the x-axis meets the experimental curve at $P$. The coordinates of $P$ will be
question: In an optics experiment, with the position of the object fixed, a student varies the position of a convex lens and for each position, the screen is adjusted to get a clear image of the object. A graph between the object distance $u$ and the image distance $v$, from the lens, is plotted using the same scale for the two axes. A straight line passing through the origin and making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the x-axis meets the experimental curve at $P$. The coordinates of $P$ will be
solution: It is possible when object kept at centre of curvature. $$ \begin{aligned} & u=v \\ & u=2 f, v=2 f . \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: A hemispherical vessel is completely filled with a liquid of refractive index $\mu$. A small coin is kept at the lowest point $(\mathrm{O})$ of the vessel as shown in figure. The minimum value of the refractive index of the liquid so that a person can see the coin from point E (at the level of the vessel) is
question: A hemispherical vessel is completely filled with a liquid of refractive index $\mu$. A small coin is kept at the lowest point $(\mathrm{O})$ of the vessel as shown in figure. The minimum value of the refractive index of the liquid so that a person can see the coin from point E (at the level of the vessel) is
solution: For the rays from coin to reach the point $E$, the refracted rays must grazing the surface, i.e. they must be incident at critical angle $\theta_c$ inside the liquid. $\mu=\frac{1}{\sin \theta_c}$ $\mu$ is minimum when $\theta_c$ is maximum. Maximum value of $\theta_c=45^{\circ}$ $\Rightarrow \mu$ has a minimum value of $\sqrt{2}$.

subject: Physics
q:
A convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and focal length $f=18 \mathrm{~cm}$ is immersed in water. The difference in focal lengths of the given lens when it is in water and in air is $\alpha \times \mathrm{f}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(refractive index of water $=4 / 3$)
question:
A convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and focal length $f=18 \mathrm{~cm}$ is immersed in water. The difference in focal lengths of the given lens when it is in water and in air is $\alpha \times \mathrm{f}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(refractive index of water $=4 / 3$)
solution:
Using the lensmaker's equation, in air:
$\frac{1}{f_{air}} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$ where $(n-1) = 0.5$ for glass with $n = 1.5$.
In water:
$\frac{1}{f_{water}} = \left(\frac{n}{n_{water}} - 1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
$= \left(\frac{1.5}{4/3} - 1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
$= \frac{1}{8}\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
Since $\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right) = \frac{2}{f_{air}} = \frac{2}{18}$,
We get $\frac{1}{f_{water}} = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{2}{18} = \frac{1}{72}$, so $f_{water} = 72$ cm.
The difference is $f_{water} - f_{air} = 72 - 18 = 54$ cm.
Therefore $\alpha = \frac{54}{18} = 3$.

subject: Physics
q: A telescope of aperture $3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$ diameter is focused on a window at $80 \mathrm{~m}$ distance fitted with a wire mesh of spacing $2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$. Given: $\lambda=5.5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$, which of the following is true for observing the mesh through the telescope?
question: A telescope of aperture $3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$ diameter is focused on a window at $80 \mathrm{~m}$ distance fitted with a wire mesh of spacing $2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$. Given: $\lambda=5.5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$, which of the following is true for observing the mesh through the telescope?
options: Yes, it is possible with the same aperture size.
Possible also with an aperture half the present diameter.
solution:
Given : $d=3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
$$
\lambda=5.5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}
$$
Limit of resolution, $\Delta \theta=\frac{1.22 \lambda}{d}$
$$
=\frac{1.22 \times 5.5 \times 10^{-7}}{3 \times 10^{-2}}=2.23 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{rad}
$$
At a distance of $80 \mathrm{~m}$, the telescope is able to resolve between two points which are separated by $2.23 \times 10^{-5} \times 80 \mathrm{~m}$ $=1.78 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: Very large size telescopes are reflecting telescopes instead of refracting telescopes.
Statement 2: It is easier to provide mechanical support to large size mirrors than large size lenses.
question:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: Very large size telescopes are reflecting telescopes instead of refracting telescopes.
Statement 2: It is easier to provide mechanical support to large size mirrors than large size lenses.
options: Statement-1 is true and Statement-2 is false.
Statement-1 is false and Statement-2 is true.
Statement-1 and statement-2 are true and Statement-2 is correct explanation for statement-1.
Statements-1 and statement-2 are true and Statement-2 is not the correct explanation for statement-1.
solution: One side of mirror is opaque and another side is reflecting this is not in case of lens hence, it is easier to provide mechanical support to large size mirrors than large size lenses. Reflecting telescopes are based on the same principle except that the formation of images takes place by reflection instead of refraction.

subject: Physics
q:
Three rays of light, namely red R, green (G) and blue (B) are incident on the face PQ of a right angled prism PQR as shown in figure.

The refractive indices of the material of the prism for red, green and blue wavelength are 1.27,1.42 and 1.49 respectively. The colour of the ray(s) emerging out of the face PR is :
question:
Three rays of light, namely red R, green (G) and blue (B) are incident on the face PQ of a right angled prism PQR as shown in figure.

The refractive indices of the material of the prism for red, green and blue wavelength are 1.27,1.42 and 1.49 respectively. The colour of the ray(s) emerging out of the face PR is :
solution:
Assuming that the right angled prism is an isoceles prism, so the other angles will be 45° each.

⇒ Each incident ray will make an angle of 45° with the normal at face PR.

⇒ The wavelength corresponding to which the incidence angle is less than the critical angle, will pass through PR.

⇒θC= critical angle

⇒θC=sin-11μ

⇒ If θC≥45°

the light ray will pass

⇒θCRed=sin-111.27=51.94°

Red will pass.

⇒θCGreen=sin-111.42=44.76°

Green will not pass

⇒θCBlue=sin-111.49=42.15°

Blue will not pass

⇒ So only red will pass through PR.

subject: Physics
q: Distance between an object and three times magnified real image is 40 cm. The focal length of the mirror used is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: Distance between an object and three times magnified real image is 40 cm. The focal length of the mirror used is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
For a mirror with magnification m = -3 (real image), object and image distances satisfy |u| + |v| = 40 cm.
Since m = v/u = -3, we have v = -3u, giving u + 3u = 40, so u = 10 cm and v = -30 cm.
Using mirror formula: $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{-30} = \frac{3-1}{30} = \frac{1}{15}$
Hence f = -15 cm.

subject: Physics
q: A ray of light is incident from a denser to a rarer medium. The critical angle for total internal reflection is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iC</mtext></msub></math> and Brewster's angle of incidence is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iB</mtext></msub></math>, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mtext>sin</mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iC</mtext></msub></mrow><mrow><mtext>sin</mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iB</mtext></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">η</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mtext>.</mtext><mn>28</mn></math>. The relative refractive index of the two media is
question: A ray of light is incident from a denser to a rarer medium. The critical angle for total internal reflection is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iC</mtext></msub></math> and Brewster's angle of incidence is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iB</mtext></msub></math>, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mtext>sin</mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iC</mtext></msub></mrow><mrow><mtext>sin</mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi><mtext>iB</mtext></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">η</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mtext>.</mtext><mn>28</mn></math>. The relative refractive index of the two media is
solution:
μ1: refractive index of rarer medium

μ2: retractive index of denser medium

sinθC=μ1μ2

tanθB=μ1μ2

sin θ C =tan θ B

sin θ C sin θ B = tan θ B sin θ B = 1 cos θ B =1.28

tan θ B = μ 1 μ 2 = 1− ( 1 1.28 ) 2 ( 1 1.28 )

=0.8

subject: Physics
q: The driver sitting inside a parked car is watching vehicles approaching from behind with the help of his side view mirror, which is a convex mirror with radius of curvature $\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{~m}$. Another car approaches him from behind with a uniform speed of $90 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$. When the car is at a distance of 24 m from him, the magnitude of the acceleration of the image of the car in the side view mirror is ' a '. The value of 100 a is _______ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$.
question: The driver sitting inside a parked car is watching vehicles approaching from behind with the help of his side view mirror, which is a convex mirror with radius of curvature $\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{~m}$. Another car approaches him from behind with a uniform speed of $90 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$. When the car is at a distance of 24 m from him, the magnitude of the acceleration of the image of the car in the side view mirror is ' a '. The value of 100 a is _______ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$.
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{uf}}{\mathrm{u}-\mathrm{f}}=\frac{-24 \cdot 1}{-24-1}=\frac{24}{25} \\
& \mathrm{~m}=\frac{-\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}}=-\frac{24}{25(-24)}=\frac{1}{25} \\
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\
& \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{I}}=-\mathrm{m}^2 \cdot \mathrm{v}_0=\frac{-1}{(25)^2} \cdot 25=\frac{-1}{25}
\end{aligned}$

Diff.
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{-1}{\mathrm{v}^2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)+\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}^2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)=0\left[\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{i}} ; \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{v}_0\right] \\
& \frac{+2}{\mathrm{v}^3} \cdot\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{t}}\right)^2-\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}^2} \cdot \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{L}}-\frac{2}{\mathrm{u}^3} \cdot\left(\mathrm{v}_0\right)^2+\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}^2} \cdot \mathrm{a}_0=0 \\
& \frac{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{I}}}{\mathrm{v}^2}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{v}^3} \cdot \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{I}}^2-\frac{2}{\mathrm{u}^3} \cdot \mathrm{v}_0^2 \\
& \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{I}}^2-\frac{2 \mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{u}^3} \cdot \mathrm{v}_0^2 \\
& =\frac{2 \cdot 25}{24} \cdot \frac{1}{25} \cdot \frac{1}{25}-\frac{2}{(24)^3} \cdot \frac{24}{25} \cdot \frac{24}{25} \cdot 25 \cdot 25 \\
& \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{2}{24.25}-\frac{2}{24} \\
& \mathrm{a}_1=\frac{2}{24} \cdot \frac{-24}{25}=\frac{-2}{25} \\
& 100 a_1=\frac{2}{25} \times 100=8
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A hemispherical glass body of radius 10 cm and refractive index 1.5 is silvered on its curved surface. A small air bubble is 6 cm below the flat surface inside it along the axis. The position of the image of the air bubble made by the mirror is seen :
question: A hemispherical glass body of radius 10 cm and refractive index 1.5 is silvered on its curved surface. A small air bubble is 6 cm below the flat surface inside it along the axis. The position of the image of the air bubble made by the mirror is seen :
solution:
Mirror image

1v+1u=1f⇒1v+1-4=1-5

1v=120
v=20

So, it is 30 cm below flat surface.

Now, After refraction

v'=30μ=301.5=20 cm

subject: Physics
q:
The light rays from an object have been reflected towards an observer from a standard flat mirror, the image observed by the observer are :-

A. Real

B. Erect

C. Smaller in size than object

D. Laterally inverted

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
The light rays from an object have been reflected towards an observer from a standard flat mirror, the image observed by the observer are :-

A. Real

B. Erect

C. Smaller in size than object

D. Laterally inverted

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution: Image formed by a plane mirror is always virtual, laterally inverted, erect image and of the same size as the object.

subject: Physics
q: The focal length of the objective and the eyepiece of a telescope are $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. If the telescope is focussed for distinct vision on a scale distant $2 \mathrm{~m}$ from its objective, then its magnifying power will be :
question: The focal length of the objective and the eyepiece of a telescope are $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. If the telescope is focussed for distinct vision on a scale distant $2 \mathrm{~m}$ from its objective, then its magnifying power will be :
solution:
Given: $\mathrm{f}_0=50 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{d}=25 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{u}_0=-200 \mathrm{~cm}$
Magnification $\mathrm{M}=$ ?
As $\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_0}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_0}$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_0}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_0}=\frac{1}{50}-\frac{1}{200}=\frac{4-1}{200}=\frac{3}{200}
$$
or $\mathrm{v}_0=\frac{200}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
Now $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{d}=-25 \mathrm{~cm}$
From, $\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& -\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}-\frac{1}{v_e} \\
& =\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{25}=\frac{6}{25}
\end{aligned}
$$
or, $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{-25}{6} \mathrm{~cm}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Magnification } \mathrm{M}=\mathrm{M}_0 \times \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{e}} \\
& =\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{\mathrm{u}_0} \times \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{-200 / 3}{200} \times \frac{-25}{-25 / 6} \\
& =-\frac{1}{3} \times 6=-2
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Two identical thin biconvex lenses of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> are in contact with each other. The space between the lenses is filled with a liquid of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math>. The focal length of the combination is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: Two identical thin biconvex lenses of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> are in contact with each other. The space between the lenses is filled with a liquid of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math>. The focal length of the combination is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
For convex lenses, let radius of curvature is R. Then,

115 cm=1.5-11R-1-R

⇒1R=115 ⇒R=15 cm

Now for concave lens,

1fconcave=1.25-11-R-1R

=14×-2R=-130

1feff=115+115-130=-110

⇒feff=-10 cm

subject: Physics
q: Five persons $\mathrm{P}_{1}, \mathrm{P}_{2}, \mathrm{P}_{3}, \mathrm{P}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{5}$ recorded object distance $(\mathrm{u})$ and image distance (v) using same convex lens having power +5 D as $(25,96),(30,62),(35,37),(45,35)$ and $(50,32)$ respectively. Identify correct statement
question: Five persons $\mathrm{P}_{1}, \mathrm{P}_{2}, \mathrm{P}_{3}, \mathrm{P}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{5}$ recorded object distance $(\mathrm{u})$ and image distance (v) using same convex lens having power +5 D as $(25,96),(30,62),(35,37),(45,35)$ and $(50,32)$ respectively. Identify correct statement
options: Readings recorded by $\mathrm{P}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ persons are incorrect
Readings recorded by $P_{4}$ and $P_{5}$ persons are incorrect
Readings recorded by all persons are correct
Readings recorded by $\mathrm{P}_{3}$ person are incorrect
solution:
For a convex lens with power $P = +5$ D, the focal length is $f = 20$ cm.
The lens formula is $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = 0.05$ cm$^{-1}$. Checking each measurement:
$P_1$: $\frac{1}{25} + \frac{1}{96} = 0.0504$ Correct;
$P_2$: $\frac{1}{30} + \frac{1}{62} = 0.0494$ Correct;
$P_3$: $\frac{1}{35} + \frac{1}{37} = 0.0556$ Incorrect;
$P_4$: $\frac{1}{45} + \frac{1}{35} = 0.0508$ Correct;
$P_5$: $\frac{1}{50} + \frac{1}{32} = 0.0512$ Correct.
Only $P_3$'s readings do not satisfy the lens formula.

subject: Physics
q: Two light waves having the same wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> in vacuum are in phase initially. Then the first wave travels a path <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> through a medium of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>while the second wave travels a path of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> through a medium of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. After this the phase difference between the two waves is:
question: Two light waves having the same wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> in vacuum are in phase initially. Then the first wave travels a path <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> through a medium of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>while the second wave travels a path of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> through a medium of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. After this the phase difference between the two waves is:
solution:
The optical path between any two points is proportional to the time of travel.

The distance traversed by light in a medium of refractive index μ in time t is given by d=vt .......(i)

Where v is velocity of light in the medium. The distance traversed by light in a vacuum in this time,

s=ct

=c×dv   [from equation (i)]

=dcv=μd .....(ii) (Sinceμ=cv)

This distance is the equivalent distance in vacuum and is called optical path.

Here, optical path for first ray =n1L1

Optical path for second ray =n2L2

Path difference =n1L1-n2L2

Now, phase difference =2πλ×path difference

=2πλ×n1L1-n2L2

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens, of focal length 30 cm, a concave lens of focal length 120 cm, and a plane mirror are arranged as shown. For an object kept at a distance of 60 cm from the convex lens, the final image, formed by the combination, is a real image, at a distance of:
question: A convex lens, of focal length 30 cm, a concave lens of focal length 120 cm, and a plane mirror are arranged as shown. For an object kept at a distance of 60 cm from the convex lens, the final image, formed by the combination, is a real image, at a distance of:
solution:
From lens formula for convex lens, we have
1f=1v-1u
⇒130=1v+160
⇒160=1v

Applying lens formula to concave lens, we have 
1f=1v-1u
⇒1-120=1v-140, ∵u=+40 cm
⇒v=60 cm

Virtual object 10 cm behind plane mirror.
Hence real image 10 cm in front of mirror.
Hence 60 cm from convex lens.

subject: Physics
q: A small bulb is placed at the bottom of a tank containing water to a depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>7</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The refractive index of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. The area of the surface of water through which light from the bulb can emerge out is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A small bulb is placed at the bottom of a tank containing water to a depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>7</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The refractive index of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. The area of the surface of water through which light from the bulb can emerge out is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Apply Snell's law, sin⁡90°sin⁡C=μ

⇒sinC=1μ⇒RR2+h2=34



Squaring, 16R2=9R2+9h2

⇒7R2=9h2⇒R=37h=37×7=3 m

The area of the circle is, A=πR2=π32 m2⇒α=9

subject: Physics
q: A vessel of depth d is half filled with oil of refractive index n1 and the other half is filled with water of refractive index n2. The apparent depth of this vessel when viewed from above will be-
question: A vessel of depth d is half filled with oil of refractive index n1 and the other half is filled with water of refractive index n2. The apparent depth of this vessel when viewed from above will be-
solution:
The apparent depth formula is given by n=Real depthApparent depth.

The apparent depth in this case is given by 

d'=d1n1+d2n2⇒d'=d2(1n1+1n2)⇒d'=d2(n1+n2n1n2)

subject: Physics
q: A thin prism <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> with an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and made of glass of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>54</mn></math> is combined with another prism <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> made from glass of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>72</mn></math> to produce dispersion without average deviation. The angle of prism <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
question: A thin prism <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> with an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and made of glass of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>54</mn></math> is combined with another prism <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> made from glass of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>72</mn></math> to produce dispersion without average deviation. The angle of prism <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
solution:
For no average deviation,

δ1+δ2=0⇒Aμ1-1-Bμ2-1=0 

⇒ 6° (1.54 – 1) = θ(1.72 – 1)

⇒θ=6°×0.540.72

=4.5°
